title: Persistent Connections, Pipelining, Ordering, Reuse, and Closure · Language Lens: Desynchronization Close
summary: A server dispatches the next request before consuming the current body, writes responses out of order, reuses connections after framing errors, and waits forever for idle peers. Planned artifact: Add and verify the desynchronization close increment in persistent-connection request and response state machine. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Close and discard connection state after ambiguous framing, parse failure, incomplete output, unexpected residual bytes, or state disagreement.
Bound requests per connection, idle time, lifetime, buffered residual data, and closure reasons without truncating active messages.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Desynchronization Close · Theory Desynchronization Close
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 8273 in a privacy-sensitive proxy translating between two parser implementations. Build deterministic Go evidence to preserve HTTP/1.1 response order and reject reuse after incomplete or ambiguous input; preserve this operating constraint: diagnostics must omit credentials and attacker-controlled metric labels; then reject a protocol transition while optimistic bytes are buffered and prevent desynchronization. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Desynchronization Close contract for the supplied data.
